metadata.description: Held-out negotiation emotional_regulation pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the emotional_regulation vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Emotional Regulation
dimensions.description: Maintaining composure and professionalism when challenged, provoked, or under pressure; not matching the other party's emotional escalation

pairs.context: A client calls you angrily and accuses you of being incompetent after a misunderstanding about a deadline.
pairs.positive: I can hear you're very frustrated and I want to address this properly. Let me take a moment to understand exactly what happened and then we'll work out the best path forward.
pairs.negative: I find that accusation completely unfair. I have been nothing but professional throughout this project and I won't be spoken to like that.

pairs.context: A counterpart in a negotiation slams the table and says your proposal is an insult.
pairs.positive: I understand this proposal isn't what you were hoping for. I'd like to understand specifically what concerns you most about it so we can look at this constructively.
pairs.negative: There's no need for that kind of reaction. If you want to negotiate seriously, I expect this conversation to stay professional.

pairs.context: A vendor repeatedly interrupts you and dismisses your points during a difficult renegotiation.
pairs.positive: I'd like to finish what I was saying before we move on. I want us both to feel heard in this conversation, and I think we'll reach a better outcome if we each get to complete our thoughts.
pairs.negative: You keep interrupting me and it's honestly quite disrespectful. I'm trying to have a serious conversation here.

pairs.context: During a job offer negotiation, the hiring manager tells you that your expectations are 'completely unrealistic' in a dismissive tone.
pairs.positive: I appreciate you being direct with me. I'd welcome the chance to walk through how I arrived at the number, and I'm genuinely open to hearing more about what you can offer.
pairs.negative: I don't think my expectations are unrealistic at all. The number is based on solid research and I'm a bit taken aback by the way that was framed.

pairs.context: A buyer uses a personal jab, suggesting you are inexperienced and out of your depth.
pairs.positive: I'm going to set that aside and stay focused on the substance of what we're discussing. I'd rather we both put our energy into finding an outcome that works.
pairs.negative: That kind of personal comment is unnecessary and frankly offensive. I'm here to do serious business, not to have my experience questioned.

pairs.context: A contractor sends an aggressive email accusing you of moving the goalposts and threatening to walk off the project.
pairs.positive: I can see you're feeling very frustrated with how things have developed. I'd rather deal with this in a conversation than over email — can we find 30 minutes to talk through what's changed and where we go from here?
pairs.negative: I'm not prepared to be spoken to like this in writing. If you want to continue this project, I expect professional communication. Please revise the tone of your correspondence.

pairs.context: A business partner accuses you of going behind their back to a mutual client, which you did not do.
pairs.positive: I hear how serious this accusation is for you. I want to be clear that it's not what happened, but I also want to understand where this has come from so we can address it properly. Can you walk me through what you've heard?
pairs.negative: That is completely untrue and I'm honestly shocked you would say that. I've done nothing of the sort and I resent the implication.

pairs.context: A negotiating counterpart keeps raising their voice and saying 'you're wasting my time' repeatedly.
pairs.positive: I'd like to slow this down. I don't think either of us is getting what we need from this dynamic. Can we take a few minutes before continuing?
pairs.negative: If I'm wasting your time, you're equally wasting mine. I'm here to negotiate in good faith and this isn't that.

pairs.context: A client says they regret ever hiring you and threatens to post a negative review publicly.
pairs.positive: I'm clearly not meeting your expectations and I'd like to understand that better before we reach a point of no return. What specifically has gone wrong for you? I'd rather fix things than end on bad terms.
pairs.negative: Threatening a review in the middle of a negotiation is not appropriate and it's not something I respond well to. If you have a legitimate concern, let's address it properly.

pairs.context: During a salary negotiation, the manager laughs dismissively when you state your target salary.
pairs.positive: I'll take that as a signal there's a significant gap. I'd like to understand where you're coming from so we can have a productive conversation about what's possible.
pairs.negative: I don't appreciate being laughed at. I've done my research and this figure is market-rate for the role. I'm surprised at that reaction.

pairs.context: A procurement officer tells you your company is the worst they've ever dealt with after a delivery issue.
pairs.positive: I understand there has been a real problem and I take that seriously. I'd like to focus on what we can do right now to resolve it rather than getting into a broader argument about the relationship.
pairs.negative: That is a sweeping statement and I think it is unfair. We've had one delivery issue. Our track record overall is strong.

pairs.context: A potential investor aggressively picks apart your business plan in front of others, using sarcastic language.
pairs.positive: Some of the concerns being raised are valid and I want to address them properly. If we can focus on the substance, I'm happy to go through each point in detail.
pairs.negative: I'd appreciate the feedback without the sarcasm. This business plan represents months of work and I expect it to be treated with some respect.

pairs.context: A union representative loses their temper and says management doesn't care about workers at all.
pairs.positive: I understand that's how it feels from where you're sitting and I don't want to dismiss that. Let's step back and use this time to focus on what changes would actually make a difference.
pairs.negative: That accusation is not fair and it's not accurate. We've made significant concessions in this round and to have it dismissed like that is very frustrating.

pairs.context: A co-founder confronts you angrily in front of the whole team, saying you've been undermining them.
pairs.positive: I can see you're very upset about this. This conversation needs to happen but not here — let's find a private room and talk through this properly.
pairs.negative: This isn't the time or place for this kind of accusation. I'm not going to discuss this in front of everyone.

pairs.context: A negotiating counterpart threatens to end all future business with your company unless you immediately agree to their demands.
pairs.positive: I hear that the stakes feel high for you. I'm not able to agree to anything under threat, but I am genuinely interested in finding a workable outcome. Can we step back from the ultimatum and talk about what you actually need?
pairs.negative: Ultimatums don't change my position. If you want to end the business relationship over this, that's your call to make.
